title: Programming Pearls
author: Jon L. Bentley
subtitle: 2nd Edition
description: A collection of 15 columns originally published in Communications of the ACM, each a pearl on the craft of programming. Bentley — AT&T researcher and ACM columnist — uses real-world problems to illustrate the power of thinking before coding, choosing the right data structures, writing correct programs before fast ones, and the disciplined art of problem-solving. The columns span algorithm design, verification, performance tuning, and estimation.
keyIdeas: Think before you code — the hardest and most important part of programming is understanding the problem
The right data structure is worth more than a thousand optimizations — hashing, binary search trees, and priority queues each unlock elegant solutions
Write correct programs before you write fast programs — clarity and correctness first, performance second
Verification is part of programming, not an afterthought — assertions, testing, and formal proof each have their place
Divide and conquer, sorting, and searching are fundamental algorithmic tools that apply across every domain
Space-time trade-offs are real — trading memory for speed or vice versa is a legitimate and powerful design decision
Estimation matters — knowing how to approximate run time, memory use, and complexity is a professional skill
Set representation is a recurring theme — choosing the right representation (bit vectors, sorted arrays, hash tables) changes complexity from O(n²) to O(n)
keyTakeaways: Spend time understanding the problem thoroughly before writing any code — the best programs come from the best problem definitions
A binary search on a sorted array beats a hash table when the data is static and lookups dominate
Hashing with a good hash function and collision resolution turns O(n) lookups into O(1)
Arrays are the simplest data structure — sort them once, then binary search is nearly free
Writing a program that is correct is more valuable than writing one that is fast but subtly wrong
Performance tuning comes last: first get it right, then get it clean, then get it fast
Debugging is twice as hard as writing the code — write clever code only if you are as clever as the person who must debug it
Estimate before you optimize — know the cost of your data structure choices in time and space
whoShouldRead: Every working programmer who wants a deeper understanding of fundamental algorithmic thinking
Developers preparing for technical interviews — the column format makes concepts memorable
Intermediate programmers who have learned syntax but not yet internalized problem-solving craft
Computer science students who want the intuition behind algorithm design beyond textbook proofs
Seasoned engineers who want a reminder of the disciplined art of thinking before coding
